Okay, so one more thing I'm trying to distance to now, everything so far this morning has been understanding your core origin story, right? And that's typically the first thing I'm telling something that's on a webinar, I'm telling that story, my first big attempt to knock down the big domino. But what happens after that is people have other concerns and other things come up, and so you can go and try to combat every single concern like most people do, or you can get really good at the story selling process where you can tell stories to kind of knock all those things down while you're doing it, which is kind of the whole point of the perfect webinars we go through. Now the first thing that I want you guys to understand is that in all of your customers, in all of my customers and everybody's customers, this is how, let me step back. So the goal of all of our stories is to create belief in somebody, right? And usually though, they have some belief pattern that's already happening, they already believe in it, right? And this is their belief, and we're trying to get them to shift like that vehicle, that belief, that thing you have is not the right vehicle. We need to change your belief to something over here, right? And so you could think about that, right? This way first, like these are the beliefs of our customers, right? They have these beliefs, and I call them chains of beliefs, which sometimes beliefs can be positive, sometimes they can be negative, right? But for our intents and purposes, typically we're looking at like whatever our customers' beliefs are aren't right, and so we had to help break those chains so we can give them the truth and help free them from whatever it is so they can get the results of our vehicle. So everyone's got these beliefs, okay? Now how do we create our beliefs? What happens if you look at it? Somewhere in your life, you're sitting here, you're going along, everything's fine, and then you have some kind of experience, right? So something happened to you, somebody punched you, or you tried to do something, it didn't work and it failed, or whatever that thing might be. There's something happening, you had this experience, and then experience led to a story you started telling yourself. Here's the story, there's this book, and that story became a belief, right? So if you guys have ever told me Robin's speaker, a lot of motivational people, they always talk about like, what's the story you tell yourself? Okay, that story is the thing that drives your whole life, your whole future, and you have to change that story if you want to change your life. So I got to realize like, what is the story that is holding my customers back, and they come back from there, and then what is the experience they had? So this is kind of an understanding the process. Someone has an experience, from that experience they create a story, from that story they create a belief pattern, okay? So that process is true in positive things and negative things. So think about just some of the beliefs that you hold true. It could be religious beliefs, it could be fitness beliefs, it could be business beliefs, whatever those things might be, and start thinking about like, what's the belief you have? So let's say, what's the good one I can say? I have a belief that getting in a cryosana three days a week is like the coolest thing in the whole world, right? I believe that, I love it, and so much so I bought one and have it in my house and we do that, right? So why do I believe that? Well, something happened. I met guys like Anthony had cool videos I'm gonna cryosana, I'm like, that looks so cool, I don't know what that is, I want one. And then I heard Tony Robbins had one and I was so excited, so I went to San Diego one day and Dave took me, where Dave's at, took me and we actually froze, and I don't know if it did anything but it felt really, really cool. I was like, this is awesome. Like this is like the coolest thing in the world. So I told myself this story, like if I really wanna be happy, I have to have one of these things in my house, I can do it all the time, and it became a belief, like if I don't have this, I'm gonna be miserable, and that belief came so real that I went out there and actually ended up getting one. So that's like a weird one, but it could be for any of your beliefs. I think about your religious beliefs. Everyone here has some type of religious beliefs or nor do religious beliefs. It doesn't really matter, it's the same thing. Some experience happened in your life, which you created a story from that and that's why you believe what you believe. And so that's kind of the process of how things happen. So that's the first thing, the step we need to understand. So the second step here comes to the false belief patterns. What I'm gonna start doing is I wanna figure out the reasons why my customers believe things that's holding them back from buying. So the first thing I do is I come out here to the change the false belief, and I'm like what is the belief that these guys have that I have to get rid of? So if they're gonna buy your product, or whatever product, what is the belief that they're holding true that if they believe that, there's no way they're gonna give you money. So for me, a belief could be, oh, I'm okay, because I already have infusion soft. I already have infusion soft, I'm good. So I gotta be like, okay, why do they believe that? And it's like, okay, what was the experience they probably went through? So they went through experience, why do they have this, what was the experience they had? And so their experience could be like, well, I was at a Dan Kennedy event and they said infusion soft was the thing, therefore I need to have that, right? So that becomes, the second thing here is their experience is that I was at a Dan Kennedy event. He said, infusion soft's the coolest thing, so that's why I have it. So from there they build a story, like if I wanna be successful online, I have to have infusion soft, and this is the story they tell themselves. No, if I wanna be successful, I have to have it. Like Dan Kennedy said, that's the story, I know like I have to have that, okay? And so I'm looking at those are the first things I need to know, so this is their story. And then for me, I gotta look at that and say, okay, this is their story, do I have a story that helps them understand that their story is not true? And if I can find a Pifty Bridge story that trumps theirs, we're like, dang, I was wrong. Like that is better, that's different, then they're gonna get rid of their story and they're gonna take my story as their new story. And that's the magic in how this whole thing works is the third thing we're figuring out, like what is your story here? So I'll go to the network marketing industry, cause this one's the easiest to kinda think through. So network marketing, they have a false belief, like this is someone who went into network marketing one time and tried it and failed, right? So I have a belief that the only way to make money in network marketing is to bug your friends and family members. Okay, well why do they believe that? Well, I met this guy named Brandon, no, just kidding. So I had an experience, I signed up for BLA company and then I went, I got my phone out and I got my contacts and I called my mom and then my dad and then my brother and then my sister and then six phone calls my entire family hated me. So they had this horrible experience, right? So what's their story? Like that sucks, only way of running a network marketing is to get your friends and family to hate you. So that's where I'm approaching people. So like if I'm trying to sell them network marketing, they're gonna be like, dude, no, I've done that before, it's horrible, right? Like it destroyed my relationships and that's the story. And it's hard to break those stories. The reason why it's hard to break people's belief patterns because this thing is so real. So what I have to do is I have to say, okay, what a Pifty Bridge story do I have that can help that's better than that, that I can give them that'll replace their story? So I can say, no, dude, I have the same story, boom, backstory, I have the same story that you had. I was in the same spot, I understand that. But guess what I found out? I found out there's this weird thing called the internet where you can actually get people to fill out forms and then you just call the people that actually wanna make money. I tell them my whole story, I do the whole Pifty Bridge and all of a sudden I tell my story here and in my story, Trump says they're like, huh, I didn't know that was possible. Whole time I thought this was reality, but it's not true. Like this is not true, like what you told me is the truth. And as soon as they believe that, then they have a new story, all this stuff goes away and now their story is your story. That's the magic we're talking about here. So if you look at like, why does Russell tell so many stories? It's because I know like my audience, like what false we've had, I have to break through to get them to buy. I know what belief patterns like when I'm in the room here, if I want you to understand things, I have to give it a other belief pattern. He's like, watch this every single day to become a better wrestler. I'm like, cool, thanks Coach. He's like, go get your wallet. Like, what do you mean? He's like, go get your wallet. So I'm like, okay, like my idol Olympic champ could kill me with his bare hands. I'm like, okay, hold on. So I get my wallet. I grab it. I bring it over and I hand it to my coach to the wall. He opens it up, pulls out the money out of it, puts it in his pocket, hands it back and empty wallet. I was like, Coach, what are you doing? He's like, I'm teaching you a lesson that'll change your life. If I gave this tape for free, you would put it on the shelf and never would have watched it. But now you've invested in it, so now you're going to watch it and you're going to become better because of that. I was like, okay, Coach, I understand. And I did. He left. I was so scared I should have watched it and I watched it over and over and over and over again. And I became a better wrestler. And so the same thing for you. I could give you this crap for free, but guess what's going to happen? You're going to set up on the shelf and never use it. So I'm going to make you invest because if you invest it, then you're actually going to use it, just like my coach did for me. And I care about you guys that much. I'm going to let you invest in this. And all of a sudden that story is like, dude, you're right. I need an invest. I've tried before and I've had success. And all of a sudden I replace their story with mine. And that belief disappears. And now they believe they have to give me money to be successful. Domino falls down. Is that cool? All right, so the next piece of this is now that we have these, if you guys again, spend more time, go home and keep doing this, this is a bit like a like, you should have a notebook, which is like stories after story, after story. The first time I ever heard this was, Dan Kennedy had a course that he kind of went through this and he said that every single objection you ever get, you need to find a story that matches it, like every single one, it's like it's the lifetime of like building up this inventory of stories and after you have them, like it just becomes so easy. And so most people are like just hoping that in the moment when a rejection or a thing comes up, your brain will just pop it out, but it doesn't work that way. The way it pops out of your mind is because you've written it down, you know it, you've practiced, you've said that story and then it comes out all the time and you're like, oh cool, like this situation, like half the stuff I've helped, I don't really have like super organized plans, I have like a direction I'm going because I'm talking, like I feel like from you guys' stuff and then like stories that start popping out, but it's only because I've told those stories a hundred times before, like okay, boom, this will help them understand that and this will help them understand this and they're probably getting stuck here so I need to share this story, that'll help them understand that, right? So the more you're doing it, the more they seem to start popping up and just kind of, they come to you when you need them, it's the coolest thing in the world, but only if you've developed those. It's like talents, right? If you're not developing your talents, like when it's your shot to like use it, you're like an idiot, but if you're developing it and every time you're doing Facebook Live, you're just telling a story, like you're doing a blog post, you're telling a story, you're doing the podcast, you're telling like consistency of doing this over and over and over again. I managed to finish, I think I'm an episode 306 or seven or eight in my podcast, it was like 300 times I've had a chance to tell my stories over and over and over again. Facebook Live, like all these things, I'm just always doing it so that when I need it, boom, it's there and I don't have to think like, how does the story go? Like it just pops in your head when you need it. It's the coolest thing in the world. It comes from repetition. All right, so the next phase of this, now that we know these kind of things, I wanna come back to what we were talking about earlier is the big domino. So my goal again is we have this belief statement we all created earlier, and if I give them to believe this and knock down the big domino, then they have to give me money, right? That was the premise of what we talked about earlier. So my first goal, when I tell my very first, my origin story, that's my first shot at pushing this domino over. And for a lot of people to hear that, you push it over and like, boom, I'm in, sweet. And that's all it takes. Sometimes like one story is all they need to join your culture to buy your thing, whatever it might be. But for most people, like you start pushing that down, they're like, oh, this is really cool. And all of a sudden like all these false beliefs and like their entire past start jumping out, wait, wait a minute, hold on. And they start popping up and they start propping up this domino. Like, wait, well, what about this? What about this? What about it? And all these things start stopping it from knocking it over. And so you start looking at this deeper, I was like, well, what are the core beliefs that are propping it? Like as I'm pushing this domino over, like what's the feedback that keeps coming back? We found it's typically three core things. Number one is that they're freaking out because they heard your original origin story and they're like, oh, cool, this is awesome. Like funnels are the key, that's amazing. And all of a sudden like, wait, what about? And all of a sudden all these, everything else they've heard in the past starts popping up like their false beliefs about the vehicle that you're offering them. Like, wait, but I thought, I thought in student software, I thought I could do it in WordPress, I thought that all these things start popping out, all these fears, right? Or, I should say and or, they start popping out these internally. So that's cool, but like, I don't know if I can do that. Like, I'm not techie, I'm not, I failed in the past, like, I'm just me. Like, all their internal stuff starts coming about, why they don't think they can do it. And then third thing is like, all these other things like, oh, well, maybe I could do it, but there's this other thing, all these other things that are keeping them back, right? And so we kind of break it down into three things that are usually holding people back. They're false beliefs about the vehicle, they're false beliefs about themselves, and the false beliefs about external things they think might be holding them back from success. Okay, and so what I created in the book, which is kind of cool, is the next step. So after you have this inventory of stories recreated, I want you to look at all these different beliefs right here, and I want you to figure out which one of these three categories is it fit into? Is this a false belief about the vehicle? Is it a false belief about themselves? Or is the false belief about something else external that's holding them back? Okay, and I want you to go in this page and kind of put them in there, and then from that figure out, like, what's the biggest thing, what's the core thing that's actually holding them back? Okay, because there's definitely gonna be a hierarchy. Something's like, ah, it's not as big, but something's like, that's the big thing that's gonna hold them back. So what's the belief about this related to the vehicle about themselves or the external? So come back to your list right here, we're taking a minute now, and go back through and plug these things in of where they fit, which one of these three things is that false belief, and then I want you to figure out for your people, what's the biggest one, what's the core objection? They have about the vehicle, the core objection about the internal belief, and the core objection about their external belief. Does that make sense? Yeah, so for my stuff, right, so internet marketing, the vehicle is, for example, I'm trying to convince them of funnels, right? So it's like, the vehicle's funnels, so first I gotta convince them of like, well what are all the false beliefs about funnels? Okay, and I'll show you tonight, as they're my slide, like how I'm doing that, then the internal's like, okay, well, I believe in the vehicle, sweet, I'm in for that, but I don't know if I can do it, so it's like internal fear of like, I'm not techie, I'm not whatever. So then if I can convince them to that, then they're like, okay, cool, funnels is the vehicle, I believe I can actually do it, but even if I had a funnel, I don't know how to drive traffic that funnel, like that's something external, right? Or if it's in the weight loss, it could be like, all right, I believe ketosis is the thing, I actually believe that because it's magic powder I drink that tastes like candy, I could do it, but it's like, oh, but like, I don't know if I can give up like the sugars, like the other things I don't know if I can give up there, that's kind of more internal as well, but, or maybe it's like, I think I could do it, but I know my wife buys the food, and I come home at night and she's got cookies and candy for the kids, and like, I can't not eat the candy when it's sitting there, because it's so good, right? So, in fact, in my web, cause we did a webinar with Kiyosaki, so I say mine, but it doesn't have, even if I didn't, before I did a webinar with him, I just used it anyway. Someone's asked me here the questions like, I can't afford that, and I totally get that, cause I was just like you at one point, I can afford that? Remember my dad bought me this book, actually my first start in my business, it's called Rich Dad, Port Ad, have you guys read that before, Robert Kiyosaki? Oh, it's so cool. So in the book I had this huge epiphany, I was reading it, and in the first two or three chapters, he was talking about the mindset between rich people and poor people, and he said that poor people look at something and say, I can't afford that. Where's the rich, something's got a rich mindset, even though they don't have money, they look at it and say, well how can I afford that? And it's almost the same, I can't afford that, how can I afford that? But it's like changing a couple of words, it's just in the mindset. So if you're thinking like, I can't afford that, like right now you're thinking with the poor mindset, like you're never gonna succeed in this or anything like because you're just shutting it off. Where if you say, how can I afford that, it also opens up your mind and the possibility, like for example with me, I couldn't afford this thing, I was trying to buy it, and so what I did is I actually went and I bought a rake, so I think how can I afford that, I looked outside and there were leaves on the ground, so I actually went and bought a rake, and I raked my neighbor's leaves, he gave me 100 bucks, I raked 10 people's leaves, I got $1000 to invest in the course. So I realized that I couldn't afford it, but this thing I was gonna learn how it could serve other people, so I actually found somebody else that wanted it, that needed the result, and I had him buy it, he financed it for me, and then I went out and actually did all the work. In fact, a lot of our people that bought Funnel Hacks, they didn't have the money, so they called one of their buddies as a business owner, and the business owner financed it in exchange for them building a funnel, and I kinda tell a couple of stories like that. I give them ideas of like, oh, how can I afford that? And I paint some pictures. Like, yeah, it is, but it's not compared to like, in five years, it's not looking back, like $80,000 isn't that much, right? And the cool thing about money is that it replenishes every single month. So the whole like, my first mentor, Mark Joyner, and he's on an episode of Funnel Hacker TV, you see, I saw his story, but he had this concept he used to call it, the Zergarnik effect, you haven't heard that before? And the story behind it says, it was a study done in like the 60s where they found out that like a waiter or waitress would go and they would take an order and not even have to write down and they'd remember perfectly and then they'd go and they'd come, they'd get the order and then two seconds later, they have completely forgotten what the thing was. But until they completed that loop, they can remember everything with like, have you ever had that waitresses? They like, they know everything, you're like, how did you remember that? And then they don't forget to, because like until the loops closed, like they can't forget it. And our brains work that way. And so like, it's all in NLP, they call it like opening and closing loops. Like you open a loop where it's like, like what's the, they drag you to the next thing. Reality shows do that. Every time it's about to end, they open some loop, they like freaks you out and you have to watch the commercials to see the next thing. Soap offers do it. It's like, all these things do this. It's like same thing. I'm trying to open these curiosity loops. It's like, I can't solve that unless I'm on the webinar. Like, go. People are always like, no one shows me my webinar. What do I do? When I'm building a presentation, whether it's a webinar, it's a Facebook live, whatever the thing is where I'm selling, there's a couple of core things I'm looking for. For me, it's like the big domino. That's first I'm looking at. What's the one thing? And then I'm trying to figure out like a big domino statement. So how to blank without blank, right? We talked about it earlier today. So that gives me the first thing right here. Actually, I'm going to go here. So my one thing, so the big domino. So how to blank without blank. This is like my big domino statement. Then I'm coming down. I'm trying to get my three core secrets. Take it one, two, and three, which we just did. So now I've got this structure. And the next thing I'm looking to do is not start building on my slides. Next thing I'm trying to figure out is like what are they going to be giving me money for? What is the actual offer of what I am selling? And this is where I'll spend the next most time after these things. So the core things, before I just build anything I've got, this, these three things, and my offer slide figured out. So as I have that, I'm like, boom, I got everything I need. Now I get all motivated. I go in there and I'll bust out all the rest of the presentation, all those other things. I'll talk about this a little tomorrow. Sometimes all I do is I write this and this and this on a whiteboard. And I click record and I'll make a crap ton of money just doing that really, really fast. We'll talk about more tomorrow. But these are the core, these are the most important pieces. The one thing, three secrets, and what the offer is. So I want to focus a little time on the actual offer because that's the key of what, if they're going to buy or they're not going to buy. And so the next thing I'm creating is what we call the stack slide. And we're going to talk about the stack tonight. So I'm going to go into the whole psychology behind it right now. I have a slide that's going to give, this is the offer. This is what they're going to be buying to get into the new vehicle. You get them over there where we're trying to take them to. And this is the stack slides. This is what they're acting. You look at all business, all it comes down to is like, you're going to give me money, I'm going to give you this. And so that's what this is. When you give me this amount of money, I'm going to give you this. So I want to make sure I know exactly what that is and I'm creating this whole thing. Because a lot of times, my secrets that I create over here, the offer I'm creating is going to, like these things are weaving in together. So if I'm going to talk about traffic, then I'm going to have something in my offer related to traffic. Because I know that's a fault, like an internal belief, an external belief they have. So I need to make sure my offer has these things. So I'm looking at these like, now that we know this, we've talked about these things. Now I'm creating the offer, I'm going to figure out how are these things all going together. So I'm typically doing an offer, a stack slide, a few things. The first thing is like, what's the core thing that you're selling? And for a lot of you guys in here, it is some kind of training, right? So maybe a master class, whatever you want to call it, doesn't really matter. It's usually like the training part of your thing. And after you list out the training, then I assign a value to it. And the value is not like how much they would buy it for on the street. The value is like, and in fact, Tosh explained this last event the best, it's like, what's the value of the result they would get from that, right? So if I go through this training and it gives me a better job or I increase my yearly, how much I make a year by $30,000, and that's worth $30,000, right? If I give you a training, it helps you with the funnel, it makes you $100,000 a year, like it's worth $100,000, right? So I'm associating that. So I might say, and I don't go too high, but let's say like this one, I did 997, and that becomes the value of the actual master class or the training. Now typically when I do mine, if you notice pretty consistently, most of my trainings are like six week master classes. There's usually a six week core thing tied to whatever I'm selling, and the reason why is in the past, I did trim trainings that were like, like my first one I ever sold was called affiliate bootcamp, and it was a paid thing of 1,000 bucks, and initially I think I had eight modules in it, and then I got excited, because people, I did it right in an ask campaign for every single training call. People gave me all the questions and I like taught on that, and I kept getting more excited and more info, and I entered those eight calls, and then I'm turning into like 27 calls, and the course went on for like three and a half, four months, and I like four month mark, I started getting people asking for refunds, I'm like, what are you talking about? They're like, yeah, this thing never ends, like I want out, I want my money back. I'm like, but I'm giving you so much value, and like there's something about like finality, if you don't ever end, like people don't feel complete, and they don't feel like they got what they came for. So I started making short, and I started making like four week things, the problem with four week things, if I have like a 30 day money back guarantee, then like they get everything, and then the money back guarantee, and then they leave, I'm like, go, so I found my sweet spot, it's like six weeks, it gets them past the refund period, but then it ends, and then they're like sweet, we're done, and they have finality, and even if they haven't implemented it, they have closure, which is important in a buying decision, if they don't have that closure, like they get annoyed as well. So for me, six weeks is like sweet spot, sometimes we do eight or 10 to pay, I can certify, I think is it 10 right now? So it's not an always thing, but typically in mind about six weeks, and that's the reason why, when you guys look at the stuff I do. Okay, so that's the training portion. Any questions about that part at all? Okay, cool. Then, a lot of people, they're doing their stack, and I do this sometimes, I mean, it kind of depends on what you're creating, but for the most part, you don't want to have like training, and then more training, just tons and tons and tons of training, because that's just over, it's like school, I don't know how many of you guys struggled at school, but I did bad, I hated it, like I didn't want another class, like that's the last thing I wanted was another class. So I try to like make this, this training, this is like where all the training is happening, and you'll even see tonight when we go start going through the slides, like I don't spend a whole bunch of time going through what's gonna happen this week, we're gonna do this, and this week, I try to cover that fast, because as soon as I go into the training, like all this stuff they're gonna learn, they get tons of overwhelm, like, oh, that sounds like work. So I don't want tons of work, I want this to be like, this is the training, and in six weeks you're gonna have the thing, you're gonna have the result, we're gonna be there, it's gonna be awesome. Then I start transitioning out to like, okay, they have the training, what are the tools that I can create that is gonna facilitate this training? Okay, so for me, the tool obviously is click-funnels, like click-funnels is the tool. Obviously some of you guys don't have click-funnels, but the tool doesn't have to be software. Software is like one of the greatest tools ever, so you have software that helps facilitate the training, it's like, I think the best thing. If you don't have software, like the next things I start looking at is first off how to build some software, because it's really cheap and easy, typically, to make some kind of software. But the second thing is like, what are the things, like the byproducts of what you do right now? Okay, so like Liz Benny, when she did her webinar, she was selling her social media training, and in that process, she had to hire lawyers to build contracts and agreements with clients, all these like, these tangible things that were like tools that she had to have in her business, and that someone, if they were to sign up for this, then like, okay, I've become a social media manager, and I have to hire a lawyer to build a lot of stuff. So she said, no, I already created the tools for it. You're like, I already paid the lawyers, you didn't get all my contracts and my things, and that became the tools. So it's the shortcut that people will get because they're investing with you versus anyone else, okay? In our high ticket secrets webinar we did, we had the six week training teaching you guys how to build out a high ticket secret program, and then the tool was basically our sales script. It was like a three page, two and a half page sales script, was the actual tool. So it's like, hey, now you're not selling so high ticket things, how do you guys want my script? Like this is my proprietary way of how I do it. So it's two and a half pieces of paper, but that's the thing that people buy for, right? People bought Liz's things, they want those contracts, it saves them so much time and frustration and money, even though it's like three pieces of paper. Okay, but it's the tools. So software tools, the byproducts where you've created our tools, it's kind of thinking through like what are those tangible things they can get that makes this whole process so much easier. Like oh, cool, I'm a lawyer now, sweet, I'm in. Oh, cool, I'm throwing my own script, I'm in. Cool, I don't have to have a webmaster, I'm in. Whatever that thing might be, okay? And a lot of times you can turn these, you can take a contract and turn it into a software product really easily, like hire some of Elantz and be like I need a website, they put in their name and their client's name, they push the button and it'll generate a PDF and pop out this thing they can then print. Now something in your contract is a software, like a software generating, or a contract generating software generator or something, right? And now something the value goes up because it's software, even though it costs you 20 bucks to get done on Freelancer and it's ready to rock and roll. So anyway, it's like these my tools, like the byproducts and things like that, okay? Now what I'm looking at is we introduced, we know these false beliefs, right? And so we talked about them, we've knocked those things down, but there's still apprehension, like there's still fear, just because you told the story, they have a new story, they're still like this is their first time doing it, like so how do we, what can we create to make these things easier? And then the last thing is you gotta have something, like the biggest tool that we have on our side to make money is urgency and scarcity. Like, that's it. Like if there's nothing else you learn from this weekend, it's like urgency and scarcity is what gets people to pull a credit card out of the wallet. Without that, it's really, really hard. You'll get the hyperactives and everybody else will be like, oh, I'll do it tomorrow. Oh, like until there's the deadline, like it's really, really hard, right? So how do you create something that causes urgency and scarcity? So for ClickFunnels, we give people that sign up right now unlimited accounts. For other things, I think it's secret that people that sign up at a certain time will then have a phone call with Robbie to look at their sales script. Like there's something that's like a finite thing where there's urgency and scarcity tied into that thing. And that's usually the bonus. And I would say like, this thing's priceless because I'm losing money on it, so you don't have to associate a price. But something that's like, it's priceless. And this is gonna be the thing that gets them to like take action now. Instead of like, cool, I'm gonna put my calendar, I'll think about it, I'll talk to my spouse. Like no, don't talk to your spouse. Like do this today, it's not like, it could be gone by the time you do that. And that's the last thing. You know, my goal is always, whatever I'm selling is make sure the value here is at least 10x what the price is. So if I'm selling a $1,000 product, I wanna make sure the value adds up to over $10,000. I'm selling a $300 product over $3,000. Like 10x is what I'm trying to create. All right, then the other thing I wanna show you guys real quick, just to kind of explain the model as a whole, cause if you understand the model, this will make it, this will make it better. So a couple things, I had this conversation, I don't know if I should say names or not. All right, screwed, I'll say names, don't tell anybody, but one of my friends, Ray Higdon's, the network marketing program space, and he's like doing great, but he's at this level, he's been at this level for multiple years. He's like, Russell, how do I get to the next level we're at right now? Eric Warry, who's like the kingpin of the network market space, had a phone call last week with him, he said, Russell, I'm stuck at this spot right here, how do I get to here? And I told him the same thing. Both those guys said, cool, and I'm probably not gonna do that. And they didn't. And I'm like, so for me, I was in both those guys' situations for a long time. I was stuck anywhere from like three to $4 million a year, consistently year in and year out, like five or six or seven years in a row. And I was like, that's great money, everything's awesome, but it was so frustrating. So initially I was like, okay, I'm making $3 million a year with one business, what if I made two businesses, then I'll make six millions, I made two businesses, and both of them are going, what happened is this one's making $3 million a year, it dropped like 1.5, this one's got 1.5, and suddenly I had two businesses twice the stress, I was now making $3 million a year. I was like, crap, okay, that didn't work. What if instead of having two businesses, I had 12, and that was really the jump logically that I unfortunately made. So next year we launched 12 new companies, and what was interesting is that like, all of them kind of like went down and up and evened out to a point where we made like three and a half million dollars. I was just like, okay, 12 didn't work, maybe fifth, no, can I stop at that point? I was like, okay, maybe there's something wrong with me, like maybe human beings should only have one business, and maybe that's the answer, but I was like, when I had one, it didn't work, and I was trying to figure this out, and the time I met, someone's become a really close friend, it was Mary Ellen Tribby, and super cool lady, and her story, like I heard her back story before I ever met, and her back story, she was a marketing person, she went initially to a company called Weiss Publishing, which is a financial newsletter, and the time I think Weiss was doing, I don't know the exact numbers, like three or $4 million a year, she came in, did some magic trick, within a year they done like $80 million, and then she left there, and then she went over to Agora Publishing, and she started working, I think it was early to rise, it was a division she was in charge of, the time early to rise was like three to $4 million a year, she came in and did her magic trick, within a year they were like $60 million, and so I heard these stories, so finally I meet up for a time, I'm all excited, and I'm all nervous, and then I'm like, I have one question for you, and she's like, okay, what's that? I'm like, how do you take companies from $3 million to like $60 in a year? Because that sounds really, really awesome, like I wanna do that, and she kinda laughed for a second, she's like, okay, this is the deal, she's like, all you internet marketers, you guys are so smart at some things, it's so stupid to like these other things, she's like, what you guys do is the equivalent of putting on a Broadway play, like you go and you hire the best script writers, best screen writers in the world, you go and you practice, you audition, you get the best actors and actresses, and you do all this stuff, and because you happen to live in Boise, Idaho, you decide to do your play in Boise, Idaho, and you go and you mark it like crazy, it's like everywhere, everybody shows up, opening nights, a huge smash to success, curtains drop down, everyone's clapping, it closes up, and that night you go to bed like, ah, good, I launched my thing and it worked, you wake up the next morning, and you start writing your next play, you say, that's what you guys do over and over and over and over again, I was like, guess what I've done, man, like every month, a month and a half for the last seven years of my life is launching a new offer, new business, new thing, and like, and it wears on, like I don't know how many of you guys are in that product launch phase, like it wears on you, it breaks you down, and she said, all I do is I take people like you who have created all these assets, you have this amazing thing, and just because it works in Boise, it's gonna work in Chicago, so I take it on the road, and I take your play to Chicago, then I take it to LA, then I take it to Phoenix, and I take the show on the road, you say, that's it, I'm just taking your show on the road, and so I did it in Weiss, I did it early to Rise, and she's like, if you stop focusing on like, I'm like writing a new play every single week and learn how to focus on like taking it on the road, it'll transform everything for you, and so about that time is when, right after we had the 12 businesses, and we had this new thing called ClickFunnels we were building to help those 12 businesses be easier, and as we started doing it, Todd and me and some of the initial people kind of in there, like, I think this is something big, I think it's important, I think it's special, like let's focus on this, and try to like do it, Mary Ellen said, let's take this on the road and try it, but You think about like bands don't, like most bands, they have some hits, and then they take that show on the road for the next decade, two or three times, and I was like, okay, we gotta do that, and we gotta figure out how to do that, and so when we were doing that, I said, okay, what I'm gonna do is, I'm gonna do a webinar at least once a week, every single week, tell forever, that's the plan, unless this thing fails, and I'll stop, but like if it's working, I'm gonna keep on doing it for forever, and for me, I have like the worst entrepreneurial ADD in the world, I'm like shiny objects, and all of you guys times a thousand, like it's really hard for me, but I was like, I gotta focus, gotta focus, and so we set this first thing up, we did the webinar, and it worked, and we did it again, and we did a webinar every single week, for the first year, every single week, and beyond, and in the last two years, I said, nevermind the webinar live, probably close to 70, 75 times somewhere in there, and that's what I told Ray, so I told Eric, and so I tell all of you guys, and most people are like, that's really, really cool, but I don't wanna do a webinar every single week, like, it's kind of just automated, and there's all these things, but I wanna say, if you do this process, it's like the whole like, Apple a day keeps the doctor away, if you do this, sure, we can rationalize a thousand ways, like that are different, but there's something about this that gets you focused, gets you better, and better, and better at the thing, okay? So many, like, when we did the very first ever the Funnel Hacks webinar, it was actually, I spoke at Mike Philson's event, I did the pitch on stage, and we closed 33% of the room, which was all, I'm like, oh my gosh, I figured out how to sell ClickFunnels, people were in the back, and then the next morning, as I was leaving to go to the airport, this lady came up to me, she's like, oh, thank you for your presentation, it was so great, but the problem is like, I don't have a supplement, I feel really bad, I wish I could have bought your thing, and I was like, what do you mean? She's like, well, I'm a coach, you were talking about supplement funnels, I don't have a supplement, which is too bad, because ClickFunnels looks so cool, and I was like, no, this works for, like, this doesn't just work for supplements, I have a coaching funnel, like, here's my coaching funnel, and she's like, are you kidding me? She's like, hold on, my friend's tonight, all wanted your thing, but we couldn't buy it, so she ran into the room, grabbed two of her friends, and they all went and signed up, she ran out of three of confusing in the offer, I started pulling them out and changing things, and I fixed that, and then four hours later, I did the webinar, I get in live, and to it actually, it was actually a smaller audience, but very similar list demographics and everything, and I did that presentation, and that one we did $130,000 in sales, and I was like, holy crap, and then what I did is I exported all the questions again, looked through them all, started tweaking my slides, next day I had another presentation, I did that every single time I did a webinar, sometimes twice a day, sometimes three times a word for word, I could do it right now without any slides if you guys wanted, which is kind of fun, I did it the other day and here we were testing the microphones, I can do it word for word without Not that I've done this yet, but I'm going to after this event for sure do the weekly webinars for this thing that I'm building here. But what you're talking about is the same process, a great musician goes through, you know? You learn the basics, you work the scales, it seems at first like kind of a notness and a little bit like drudgery at first, you're cutting your teeth, but you get to a place where you can just sit down and just without even thinking about it's like muscle memory, and you can have this amazing inspiring performance, you know, and people will go to a theater and watch a master pianist, and people don't realize that that master pianist probably spent thousands and thousands of hours of like honing their craft to get to where they are, to where they can just like turn it on and then turn it off. And what you're talking about is exactly that, getting in the shed, and then, you know, you don't wait until you're a master to go out and play gigs. Like when I was growing up, I was 17 years old, I was playing in bars, I couldn't play worth a crap, you know, but you go out there and you play, and then you just get better and better, and the more you're on stage, the more you're like, hey, I'm not nervous now, like I've done this, and you just start to own it, and then next thing you know, you know, you're on this big stage in front of thousands, I mean, I've played in front of thousands of people and been like not nervous at all, just like, because I know I got it. You know, so you're talking about that, and that's what you're talking about by doing this. It's a weekly gig. It's a weekly gig where you're getting in front of your audience and you just learn how to kill it and you just can turn it on. So it's awesome, man. Well, thank you.
